summary:
The Second Dynasty of Egypt, dating approximately 2890–2686 BC, followed the formative First Dynasty and continued the consolidation of the unified Egyptian state that had emerged with the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt. The dynasty is notable for a period of internal conflict, with evidence of rival kings ruling simultaneously from different power centres — one based at Memphis in the north and another possibly at Thinis in the south — suggesting that the political unification achieved under the First Dynasty was not yet fully stable.

Several kings of the Second Dynasty are known primarily from inscriptions and seal impressions. The pharaoh Khasekhemwy, the last ruler of the dynasty, is particularly well attested and appears to have reunified the country after a period of civil strife. His burial at Abydos and at Hierakonpolis are among the largest and most elaborate of the early dynastic period. His name, meaning 'the two powers have appeared', is thought to reference the reconciliation of the cults of Horus and Seth, the two divine rivals whose conflict symbolised the dualistic nature of the Egyptian cosmos.

The Second Dynasty saw important developments in royal funerary architecture and religious practice. The large subterranean tomb complexes at Saqqara associated with this period mark a transitional stage between the mudbrick mastabas of the First Dynasty and the stone monuments of the Old Kingdom. The dynasty's end led directly into [[event:1712|the Third Dynasty of Egypt]], which inaugurated the Old Kingdom and the age of pyramid-building.
